source_authority: Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India
unit: USD billion (merchandise trade balance, negative = deficit)
data_notes: March 2026 data not yet released as of April 5, 2026 (expected mid-April 2026). Monthly figures for May, Jun, Jul, Nov 2025 are DERIVED from differences between official cumulative PIB releases — derivation noted on each entry. All derived values are fully consistent with official cumulative data. This is merchandise trade only (goods); services trade balance is NOT included. India has a structural services surplus (~$15-20B/month) that partially offsets the goods deficit.

series.source: Ministry of Commerce PIB release, released May 2025 — PIB prid=2128909. Merchandise exports $38.49Bn (+9% YoY from $35.30Bn in Apr 2024). Merchandise imports $64.91Bn (+19% YoY from $54.49Bn). Combined merch+services exports $73.80Bn (+12.70% YoY).
DERIVED: (Apr-May cumulative) minus (April alone). Apr-May merch: Exp $77.19Bn, Imp $125.52Bn (PIB prid=2136680). Apr: Exp $38.49Bn, Imp $64.91Bn. May = $38.70Bn exp, $60.61Bn imp.
DERIVED: (Apr-Jun cumulative) minus (Apr-May cumulative). Apr-Jun merch: Exp $112.17Bn, Imp $179.44Bn (PIB prid=2144870). Apr-May: Exp $77.19Bn, Imp $125.52Bn. June = $34.98Bn exp, $53.92Bn imp.
DERIVED: (Apr-Jul cumulative) minus (Apr-Jun cumulative). Apr-Jul merch: Exp $149.20Bn, Imp $244.01Bn (PIB prid=2156504). Apr-Jun: Exp $112.17Bn, Imp $179.44Bn. July = $37.03Bn exp, $64.57Bn imp.

Official PIB/Ministry of Commerce data: merchandise trade deficit narrowed to $26.49Bn in August 2025; imports $61.59Bn (fell from $64.57Bn in July). Exports $35.10Bn. Cited in Nikkei Asia citing official Ministry of Commerce release.

Official PIB release, released October 2025. Merchandise exports $36.38Bn (+6.75% YoY from $34.08Bn). Imports $68.53Bn (+16.7% YoY from $58.74Bn). Trade deficit +30.37% YoY to $32.15Bn. April-September 2025 cumulative deficit $154.98Bn (PIB prid=2179604). Key import driver: electronics, fertilisers, gold, silver.

Official PIB/MoCI data. Exports $34.38Bn (vs $38.98Bn in Oct 2024, -11.8%). Imports $76.06Bn (vs $65.21Bn in Oct 2024, +16.6%). Merchandise deficit hit ALL-TIME HIGH of $41.68Bn in October 2025. Gold imports $14.7Bn — up ~200% YoY (festive season + pre-import tariff stocking). Cited in CNBC citing Ministry of Commerce official data.

DERIVED: (Apr-Dec merch exports $330.29Bn - Dec $38.51Bn = Apr-Nov $291.78Bn) minus (Apr-Oct $254.25Bn) = Nov $37.53Bn. Similarly imports: ($578.61 - $63.55) - $451.08 = $63.98Bn. Deficit $26.45Bn. Consistent with DD News report 'India exports rise 15.5% in November 2025, trade deficit narrows' from official MoCI data.

Official PIB/MoCI data, released January 2026. Merchandise exports $38.51Bn (vs $37.80Bn in Dec 2024). Imports $63.55Bn (vs $58.43Bn in Dec 2024). Apr-Dec cumulative: Exp $330.29Bn, Imp $578.61Bn (PIB prid=2214961).

Official PIB release, released February 2026 — PIB prid=2228785. Merchandise exports $36.56Bn (vs $36.34Bn in Jan 2025). Imports $71.24Bn (vs $59.77Bn in Jan 2025). Large import surge driven by gold, oil, electronics. Apr-Jan cumulative: Exp $366.37Bn, Imp $644.82Bn.

Official PIB release, released March 2026 — PIB prid=2240674. Merchandise exports $36.61Bn (vs $36.91Bn in Feb 2025, -0.8%). Imports $63.71Bn (vs $51.33Bn, +24.1%). Apr-Feb cumulative: Exp $402.93Bn (+1.84% YoY), Imp $713.53Bn. Cumulative merchandise deficit Apr-Feb: $310.60Bn.
Not yet released as of April 5, 2026. Expected: Ministry of Commerce PIB release mid-April 2026. ACTION: Populate when released. commerce.gov.in/trade-statistics/latest-trade-figures/

notes: FY26 merchandise trade deficit widened significantly vs FY25. October 2025 set all-time high deficit of $41.7Bn driven by gold import surge (+200% YoY) in festive season. Full year FY26 cumulative (Apr-Feb): exports $402.93Bn (+1.84% YoY), imports $713.53Bn (+8.5% YoY), deficit $310.60Bn (11 months). India's trade deficit structurally driven by oil, gold, and electronics imports. Services trade surplus (~$15-17Bn/month) partially offsets goods deficit, resulting in lower current account deficit.
